turns.content: Hi, I'm working on a Rust project that needs to be memory safe. We're targeting ARMv8.5 with Memory Tagging Extension (MTE). Can you explain how MTE can help?

Absolutely! MTE is a hardware feature in ARMv8.5 that adds tagging to memory addresses, helping catch use-after-free (UAF) errors at the hardware level. This makes your Rust app more resilient without needing manual checks everywhere.
So how does MTE detect UAF?

When an address is freed, its tag gets invalidated. If you try to read or write that memory again without a valid tag, the hardware raises a fault. This catches UAF before it causes undefined behavior, which can help prevent crashes and security bugs.
That sounds great! So how do I use MTE in Rust?

Rust itself doesn't directly expose hardware extensions like MTE, but you can leverage the `hwasan` crate (hardware-assisted sanitizers). It uses the same approach as AddressSanitizer but on ARM with MTE support. You add `-Z sanitizer=hwasan` to your Cargo build flags.
Oh, so it's similar to ASAN, just hardware accelerated?

Yes! It’s a runtime check that runs alongside your app. But unlike software-only sanitizers like `rust-sanitize`, hwasan leverages the MTE hardware capabilities for faster detection of many classes of bugs.
And what about when I want to manually control memory tagging?

That’s quite advanced. You’d typically avoid manual manipulation unless you’re working on low-level memory safety code or integrating with very specific hardware features. If you need that, you might use platform-specific assembly or inline Rust FFI calls to interact with MTE registers. But for most projects, sticking with hwasan is usually simpler and safer.
Got it! So I just need to enable `hwasan` in Cargo.toml?

Exactly! That way, Rust will generate code that uses MTE hardware features automatically. Your builds will be slower and use more memory during runtime, but you’ll get early detection of UAFs and other memory bugs.

I have a solution using a custom allocator with Drop glue that manually calls hwasan::report_undefined_behavior for every pointer drop. It catches UAF in my Rust code but it's terrible performance and I can't get the tags right on ARMv8.5. Is there a better way to handle this without writing manual glue?

You are dealing with the wrong tool for the job; hwasan is a debug artifact, not a production memory safety mechanism. The correct approach on ARMv8.5 is Memory Tagging Extension (MTE) which uses hardware tags stored in tag registers rather than relying on software glue. You should look at how Rust's unsafe block and raw pointers interact with the MTE bitfields instead of trying to patch hwasan into your allocator.

Okay, so I shouldn't use hwasan for production? But my current code uses a custom allocator that wraps Box and Vec. How do I actually implement MTE-aware memory management without rewriting the entire runtime?

You don't rewrite the entire runtime; you leverage existing crates like mte-rs or similar low-level bindings that expose the MTE hardware features directly to your Rust code. Instead of wrapping Box and Vec manually, you can use these crates to intercept pointer operations at the API level while keeping standard containers for safety-critical paths where MTE tags are not required.
That sounds like I'm just adding a new layer on top of Box and Vec. Can you give me a concrete example where the Rust types interact with hardware tags without manual glue?

A concrete example is using an MTE-aware custom allocator that implements Drop glue for your own data structures, but instead of calling hwasan, you let the hardware handle the tag checks via the MTE bitfields. You can implement a trait like MyMteSafeTrait in your code to ensure all pointers go through the tagged path while keeping standard types like Box and Vec separate for non-critical paths.
I'm confused about how the tags actually get checked. Does Rust's borrow checker even see them? I keep thinking I need to manually manage the tag state in my custom allocator like I did with hwasan.

The borrow checker does not see hardware tags; those are opaque to your Rust compiler. You must implement a trait or wrapper that manages the tag lifecycle for your custom data structures, but you do this once and never again manually. The key insight is that MTE tags are checked by the CPU on every load/store instruction, so you only need software glue if you want to intercept pointers before they hit the hardware.
So I can just use a wrapper around my custom allocator and let the hardware do the heavy lifting? But what if I accidentally drop a pointer without updating the tags?

If you accidentally drop a pointer without updating tags, the CPU will detect the mismatch on the next memory access and raise an exception. You don't need manual glue for this; the hardware enforces consistency automatically. Your job is just to ensure your custom allocator's Drop implementation calls into the MTE-aware layer so that every pointer release updates the tag registers correctly.

Ah, I see it now! The hardware does the actual checking and my manual glue was just noise. So if I implement a wrapper around my custom allocator that uses mte-rs properly, Rust will never let me drop an untagged pointer because the exception happens before the program exits.

Exactly. The hardware ensures you can't have dangling tags without raising a fault immediately on access. Your wrapper just needs to ensure every pointer goes through the tagged path so that when it's dropped, the CPU knows exactly what state to expect.
